title: Romance of the Three Kingdoms / Chapter 11 — Liu, the Emperor’s Uncle, Rescues Kong Rong / Lü Bu Defeats Cao Cao Near Puyang
text:
The man who proposed a plan to break upCao Cao’s siege was a native of Qu county in Donghai commandery,Mi ZhustyledZizhong.

For generations thisMi Zhu’s family had enjoyed wealth and stature. Once whenMi Zhuhad traveled to Luoyang to trade, he was riding home in his carriage when along the road he encountered a beautiful woman, who approached and asked to ride along with him;Mi Zhupromptly got out to let the woman have the carriage to herself while he continued on foot, and even when she invitedMi Zhuto share the carriage with her and he took a seat as well, he assumed a reserved posture and never let his eyes wander. After they had traveled a few li, the woman took her leave of him. Yet just before departing, she informed him, “Truth be told, I am really the Lady of the Southern Fire Star, and I am fulfilling a command from the Supreme Deity to travel to your home to burn it; the great courtesy you have shown me has moved me to frankly inform you of this. You had better hurry home at once and remove your possessions, for I shall arrive tonight.”

And with that warning, she was gone. Greatly astonished,Mi Zhurushed back to his home and hastily moved all of his possessions out of the house. Just as the woman had claimed, that night a fire started in the kitchen and consumed the whole building.

From then on,Mi Zhuhad devoted his wealth to relieving the poor and comforting the afflicted, andTao Qianhad later recruited him as an Attendant Officer of the province.

The plan he now proposed was thus: “I am willing to personally go to Beihai commandery to askKong Rongto raise troops to reinforce us, while someone else should go toQing provinceto seek aid fromTian Kai. If the armies of these two places arrive,Cao Caowill certainly retire.”

Tao Qianagreed; he wrote out two letters to bring to the two leaders, asking his officers who was bold enough to travel to Qingzhou to ask for aid. When one man at once stepped forward to volunteer, everyone turned to look: it was a native of Guangling,Chen DengstyledYuanlong.Tao Qianfirst dispatchedChen Yuanlongto seek audience at Qingzhou and afterwards orderedMi Zhuto bring the other letter toKong Rongat Beihai, while he himself led the soldiers of Xuzhou to defend the city and guard against the assault.

ThisKong Rong, styledWenju, was not only a native of Qufu county in the Lu princely fief, he was in fact a twenty-fifth-generation descendant of Kong Qiu (Confucius) himself. His own father Kong Zhou had served as Commandant of Taishan.

Even as a youth,Kong Ronghad been clever and perceptive. When at the age of nine he had tried to go pay a visit to the governor of the capital region,Li Ying, the doorkeeper had tried to turn him away, butKong Ronghad claimed, “My family has always had an understanding with Lord Li’s family.”

Although the doorkeeper had thus admitted him,Li Yinghad challenged him, saying, “What association have your ancestors ever had with mine?”

Kong Ronghad replied, “I am a Kong; you are a Li. Didn’t Kong Qiu once consult with Li Er (Laozi) on the details of the rites? How can you say our families don’t go a long way back?”

His host had been astonished at the boy’s ready wit.

When shortly afterwards one of the palace grandees,Chen Wei, had arrived as well,Li Yinghad gestured towardsKong Rongwhile telling him, “This boy is a wonder.”

Chen Weihad replied, “Even a gifted child may grow to be gormless.”

“If that’s the case,”Kong Ronghad smoothly observed, “you must have been one of the gifted ones.”

Chen Weiand the rest had all burst into laughter, saying, “When this boy grows up, he’ll be a real treasure of the age.”

Ever since,Kong Ronghad enjoyed a certain renown. At length he was appointed asHousehold General, then was transferred to be Administrator of Beihai. He was so fond of entertaining guests and retainers that he liked to quip:

May the halls always be filled with guests

And the cups never empty of wine

For six years now he had been the Administrator, having greatly captured the hearts of the people.

On this day,Kong Rongwas seated among his guests when someone came in to report thatMi Zhuof Xuzhou had arrived. WhenKong Ronginvited him in and asked his reason for coming,Mi ZhuproducedTao Qian’s letter, adding, “Cao Caois assaulting and besieging the city most fiercely, good sir, and we hope you will deign to save us.”

“When I am such good friends withTao Gongzuand when you yourself have come all this way,Zizhong,”Kong Rongreplied, “how could I not go? But since I have no quarrel withCao Mengde, I ought to send someone to take a letter to him first to break up this dispute; if he refuses, then I will raise my troops.”

“Cao Caowould never make peace,” saidMi Zhu. “He is too certain of the power of his army.”

ThusKong Rongsplit his attention between ordering troops to be mustered and sending off the letter toCao Cao.

Yet while they were still discussing the issue, suddenly there came a report that the Yellow Turban leaderGuan Haiwas leading a band of tens of thousands of rebels, cutting their way towards Beihai. Greatly alarmed,Kong Rongat once summoned all the men and horses he had on hand and marched out of the city to confront the enemy.

Guan Hairode out to the front, saying, “I know the larders of Beihai are well-stocked. If you will but lend us ten thousand bushels of grain, we will withdraw at once. Otherwise, we’ll bust our way through the walls and moat, and neither old nor young shall be spared!”

Kong Rongchided him, saying, “I am a servant of Great Han and have been entrusted with the safety of its land. How could I give provisions away to bandits?”

Infuriated,Guan Haiwhipped his steed and whirled his sword around his head, riding straight forKong Rong. One ofKong Rong’s captains,Zong Bao, set his spear and rode out to meet the challenge, but after just a few bouts one good slash fromGuan HaisentZong Baoto the ground.Kong Rong’s soldiers panicked and fled back inside the walls, whileGuan Haisplit up his forces to besiege the city on all sides.Kong Rongwas very down-hearted andMi Zhu, who now saw no hope for the success of his mission, no longer knew what to say.

The next day, asKong Rongmounted the walls to gaze out at the landscape, the sheer strength of the rebel army seemed so vast that he began to feel twice as despondent. Suddenly he noticed a lone rider outside the city who, with leveled spear and galloping steed, charged into the enemy formation, dashing hither and thither as though he had the field all to himself. Upon reaching the base of the walls this rider called out, “Open the gate!” Yet as he did not recognize the man,Kong Rongdid not dare to open it. The bandits began to close in around the rider along the edge of the moat, but he whirled around and knocked more than a dozen of them from their horses and the rest fell back.Kong Rongquickly ordered the gate to be opened and the rider to be brought inside. The man dismounted his horse and cast aside his spear, hurried up onto the wall, and bowed low beforeKong Rong.

WhenKong Rongasked for his identity, the man replied, “I am a native of Huang county in Donglai commandery; my name isTaishi Ci, styledZiyi. My aged mother has received a great deal of your grace and regard, thus when I returned home yesterday from Liaodong to visit her and learned that these bandits were assailing the city, she advised me, ‘For all the deep kindness I have received from the Commissioner, you ought to go and save him.’ So I rode out all alone, and here I am.”

Kong Rongwas quite elated. For though he had never had the chance to meet the man in person before, he had heard many tales describingTaishi Cias a bold fighter, and asTaishi Cilived far away yet his mother lived only twenty li from Beihai,Kong Ronghad often sent agents to bring food and cloth to her; it was her gratitude from this past virtue that had inspired her to sendTaishi Cito aid him now.

Kong Rongshowed his appreciation by treating his guest with the greatest respect, making him presents of clothing and armor, along with a saddle and horse.

Taishi Ciproposed, “Lend me a thousand good soldiers and I will go out and slay those bandits.”

“Bold you may be, but the bandits have such awesome strength; you cannot go out so rashly,” saidKong Rong.

“Your past generosity towards my mother moved her to send me here,”Taishi Cireplied. “How shall I be able to face her if I cannot raise the siege? Let me settle this in one stroke.”

Kong Rongsuggested, “I had heard thatLiu Xuandeis a stalwart of our age; if we could get his help, the siege would break up on its own. If only there were someone we could send to him.”

“Commissioner, if you will compose the letter, I will take it to him at once.”

Kong Rongwas pleased, and he wrote out the letter and gave it to him.

Taishi Cithen put on his armor, mounted his steed, attached his bow and quiver to his girdle, took his spear in hand, and brought his haversack all tied and ready, and as soon as the gate was cracked open he went galloping out.

As he approached the moat, a rebel captain led some troops to intercept him. But with a few thrusts of his spear,Taishi Cislew several of the bandits and burst his way out of the siege line.

WhenGuan Hailearned that someone had left the city, he was certain it was someone going to find reinforcements; at once he led a few hundred riders to chase after the fugitive, and they soon surrounded him on all sides. YetTaishi Ciput away his spear, gripped his bow and readied his arrows, and loosed shots in all directions; not one of them failed to sent a man to the ground swift as the twang of his bowstring. The rest of the rebels dared not pursue any further.

Having gotten away,Taishi Cirode through the night to reach Pingyuan, where he asked to meet withLiu Xuande. Dispensing with pleasantries, he at once explained that Administrator Kong was surrounded at Beihai and was asking him for help, handing over the letter thatKong Ronghad written.

After reading the letter,Xuandeasked him, “And who might you be, friend?”

“I amTaishi Ci, just a nobody fromDonghai,” he replied. “I am bound toKong Rongby neither blood nor relation, neither hometown nor faction; it is sympathy alone that has brought me to share his sorrows and misfortunes as my own. Even now,Guan Haiplunders and pillages while Beihai is under siege, cut off and exhausted with no one to turn to and bound to fall any day now. And it was becauseKong Rongheard that you were a benevolent and righteous man who would save those in peril and distress that he ordered me to brave the enemy’s vanguard and break through their siege in order to come ask for your aid.”

Xuandegave a wan smile, saying, “So Administrator Kong knows there is aLiu Beiin this world, eh?”

Together withYunchangandYide, he selected three thousand skilled soldiers and set out to march for Beihai.

WhenGuan Hainoticed these new forces approaching, he personally led troops to intercept them, yet once he saw how fewXuande’s troops were, he did not think much of them.

The brothers andTaishi Ciwith them sat on their horses in the forefront of their array asGuan Haivented his anger and rode towards them.Taishi Ciwas about to advance in response, yetYunchanghad already gone forth, headed straight forGuan Hai. As the two riders clashed, a great clamor arose among the soldiers. Yet how couldGuan Haihave been a match forYunchang? After a few dozen bouts, the Green Dragon Saber rose and slashedGuan Haito the ground.

Taishi CiandZhang Feithen rode out together as well, their two spears rising as they charged into the rebel formation, andXuandesent his soldiers to sweep down upon the enemy. At the same time, from atop the wallsKong RongspottedTaishi Citearing through the rebel army alongsideGuan YuandZhang Feilike tigers let loose among sheep, riding to and fro with no one able to stand against them, and he too promptly sent his troops out of the city. Now struck from both sides, the rebel army was greatly defeated; countless numbers of them surrendered while the rest broke and scattered.

Kong Rongthen welcomedXuandeinto the city, and once the introductions were in order, he set out a great feast to offer his congratulations. He also brought inMi Zhuto meet withXuande, andMi Zhutold him aboutCao Song’s death at the hands ofZhang Kai. “NowCao Caohas let loose his soldiers in terrible plunder and they have besieged Xuzhou, causing me to come here seeking assistance.”

Xuanderemarked, “Tao Gongzuis a kindly man of high character, and it is a pity that he should suffer this wrong for no fault of his own.”

“You are a scion of the imperial family of Han,” saidKong Rong, “whileCao Caois harming the people, flaunting his strength to oppress the weak. Why not go with me to rescue them?”

“It is not that I would dare to decline. Yet since I have few troops and only a handful of captains, I fear I cannot be impetuous,” saidXuande.

“Though my desire to helpTao Gongzuarises from our old friendship, yet it is a righteous act as well. How could you be the one whose heart is not inclined towards what is right?” saidKong Rong.

Xuandereplied, “In that case,Wenju, I ask that you go on ahead while giving me time to seeGongsun Zan; I shall borrow three or five thousand troops and follow after you shortly.”

“You had better not break faith,”Kong Rongtold him.

“What manner of man think you that I am?” saidXuande. “As the sage (Confucius) himself put it, ‘All men die in the end, yet none will abide a liar.’ Whether I manage to borrow the men or not, certainly I shall myself come.”

ThusKong Ronggave his consent. He instructedMi Zhuto return to Xuzhou first to explain the situation, while he prepared for his expedition.

At this timeTaishi Citook his leave, saying, “My mother bade me come to your aid, and now happily you are safe. Now a letter has arrived from the Inspector of Yangzhou,Liu Yao, who is from the same commandery as me; he bids me come to him and I dare not remain. May we meet again.”

ThoughKong Rongpressed rewards of gold and silk upon him,Taishi Cionly returned home unwilling to accept them. When his mother saw him, she was pleased, saying, “How glad I am that you have repaid the Administrator!” And she sent him on his way to Yangzhou.

Of the departure ofKong Rong’s army we need say no more. Meanwhile,Xuandeleft Beihai and came to seeGongsun Zan, explaining the circumstances of his desire to save Xuzhou.

“Cao Caoand you have no grudge; why would you spend yourself for the sake of another?” saidGongsun Zan.

“I have promised,” he replied, “and dare not break faith.”

“Then I will lend you two thousand men, horse and foot,” saidGongsun Zan.

“I should like to borrow the services ofZhao Zilongas well,” saidXuande.

Gongsun Zanagreed to this also.

They marched away for Xuzhou, the three brothers leading their own three thousand men in the front whileZhao Zilongled the other two thousand to follow behind.

In due courseMi Zhureturned to Xuzhou, saying thatKong Ronghad gottenXuandeto agree to aid them as well. The other messenger,Chen Yuanlong, came back and reported thatTian Kaiwould also gladly lead troops from Qingzhou.Tao Qian’s heart was thus at ease.

However, even once the armies ofKong RongandTian Kaihad reached the area, dreading the fearsome forces ofCao Cao’s army, they held back to camp beneath some hills rather than dare march any closer. At the same time, whenCao Caonoticed these new had arrived, he split up his army into separate groups and was unwilling to advance to assault Xuzhou.

At length,Xuandearrived as well and went to seeKong Rong, who told him, “The enemy is very powerful andCao Caohandles his army skillfully; it is too soon to risk a battle. We had better see how he reacts before we advance our own troops.”

“Yet with the city so short of food,”Xuandereplied, “I fear they will not be able to hold out for very long. “Permit me to leave four thousand of my soldiers underYunchangandZilongwith you, whileZhang Feiand I march towardsCao Cao’s camp and make a break for Xuzhou to see if we can meet withTao Qianand consult with him.”

Only too pleased by this proposal,Kong Rongsoon combined his forces withTian Kaiin an ox-horn formation, withYunchangandZilongleading troops on either side to assist.

That same day,XuandeandZhang Feiled the remaining thousand soldiers to cut their way acrossCao Cao’s camp. Just as they were getting through, the sound of drums reverberated through the camp as a body of cavalry and infantry poured forth to face them like billowing waves. At their head was one ofCao Cao’s chief officers,Yu Jin, who reined in his horse and called out, “Who are you madmen? Where do you think you’re going?”

As soon asZhang Feinoticed him, he did not even bother with words but rode straight for him, with the two riders clashing for several bouts. Yet by thenXuandehad gestured with his twin blades for the rest of his troops to press on, andYu Jin’s troops had to break off in defeat.Zhang Feicharged ahead after them untilXuande’s men had reached the base of the city walls.

From atop the wall, the besieged saw these new troops bearing a red banner, with “Liu Xuandeof Pingyuan” written out in white.Tao Qianordered them to open the gate at once. OnceXuandehad entered the city,Tao Qianreceived him graciously and together they proceeded to the government office. After the initial ceremonies,Tao Qianprepared a feast for his new guests, seeing to the needs of the soldiers as well.

WhenTao Qianwitnessed how majestic and regal an appearance and how dignified in speechXuandewas, he felt so overjoyed that he at once orderedMi Zhuto hand over toXuandehis own seal of office as Inspector of Xuzhou, offering to yield his role.

“Sir, what’s all this?” askedXuandequite startled.

Tao Qiantold him, “In a time when the realm has been stirred up into turmoil and the royal sway no longer holds, you as a scion of the Han imperial family ought to devote yourself to supporting the altars of state. This old man is getting on in years and has no talents to speak of, and it is my wish to yield the governorship of Xuzhou to you. You ought not decline; I myself shall write the petition and send a memorial to the court.”

Xuandeleft his mat and twice bowed low, saying, “Scion of the Han court I may be, but my merit is small and my virtue meager; I doubt even my fitness to be Chancellor of Pingyuan. It was for the sake of the greater good that I came here to aid you; surely you are only saying this because you thoughtLiu Beisought to swallow up your domain? May blessed Heaven abandon me if I ever had such a thought!”

“But this is my genuine belief,” saidTao Qian.

Yet though time and again he offered to yield,Xuandecould not bear to accept it.

ThenMi Zhustepped in to remark, “The enemy soldiers are still beneath our walls; now is the time to discuss how to achieve their withdrawal. We can discuss this question of yielding at a more peaceful time.”

Xuandesaid, “I ought to write toCao Caoto encourage him to raise the siege. If he refuses, there will be time enough to fight.”

Thus orders were sent out to the three camps to restrain their soldiers and not to move, while an envoy was sent bearing a letter to visitCao Cao.

It happened thatCao Caowas holding a council with his generals when a messenger with a war letter was announced. The letter was brought in and handed to him and, when he had opened and looked at it, he found it was fromLiu Bei.

The letter went something like this:

“Since meeting you outside the pass, fate has assigned us to different quarters of the world and I have not been able to pay my respects to you. Touching the death of your noble father, the Marquis, it was owing to the vicious nature ofZhang Kaiand due to no fault ofTao Gongzu. Now while the remnant of theYellow Turbansis disturbing the provinces andDong Zhuo’s partizans have the upper hand in the capital, I would that you, illustrious Sir, would regard the critical position of the Court rather than your personal grievances and so divert your forces from the attack onXu provinceto the rescue of the State. Such would be for the happiness of that city and the whole world.”

Cao Caogave vent to a torrent of abuse. “Who is thisLiu Beithat he dares write and exhort me? Beside, he means to be satirical.”

He issued orders to put the bearer of the letter to death and to press on the siege. ButGuo Jiaremonstrated, “Liu Beihas come from afar to helpTao Qianand he is trying the effect of politeness before resorting to arms. I pray you, my master, reply with fair words that his heart may be lulled with a feeling of safety. Then attack with vigor and the city will fall.”

Cao Caofound this advice good, so he spared the messenger telling him to wait to carry back his reply. While this was going on a horseman came with news of misfortune.Lü Buhad made a raid onYan province.

WhenLi JueandGuo Si, the two partizans ofDong Zhuo, succeeded in their attack on the capitalLü Buhad fled toYuan Shu, who however looked askance at him for his instability, and refused to receive him. Then he had triedYuan Shao, who had made use of him in an attack uponZhang YaninChangshan. But his success filled him with pride and his arrogant demeanor so annoyed the other commandants thatYuan Shaowas on the point of putting him to death. To escape this he had gone away toZhang Yang, who accepted his services.

About this timePang Shu, who had been protectingLü Bu’s family since his disappearance, restored them to him, which deed angeredLi JueandGuo Siso that they putPang Shuto death and wrote toLü Bu’s protector to serve him the same. To escape thisLü Buonce again had to flee and this time joined himself toZhang Miao.

He arrived just asZhang Miao’s brother was introducingChen Gong. Gong said toZhang Miao, “The disrupture of the Empire has begun and warriors are seizing what they can. It is strange that you, with all the advantages you enjoy, do not strike for independence.Cao Caohas gone on an expedition against the east leaving his own district defenseless.Lü Buis one of the fighting men of the day. If you and he together attacked and gotYan provinceyou could then proceed to the dominion.”

Zhang Miaowas pleased and resolved to try. SoonLü Buwas in possession ofYan provinceand its neighborhood, all but three small departments, which were desperately defended.Cao Renhad fought many battles but was always defeated and the messenger with the evil tidings had come from him asking help.

Cao Caowas greatly disturbed by this and said, “If my own city be lost I have no home to return to. I must do something at once.”

“The best thing would be to become friends withLiu Beiat any cost and return toYan province,” saidGuo Jia.

Then he wrote toLiu Bei, gave the letter to the waiting messenger and broke camp. The news that the enemy had left was very gratifying to theGrand Administrator, who then invited his various defenders into the city and prepared banquets and feasts in token of his gratitude.

At one of these, when the feasting was over, he proceeded with his scheme of retirement in favor ofLiu Bei. Placing him in the seat of highest honor he bowed before him and then addressed the assembly.

“I am old and feeble and my two sons lack the ability to hold so important an office as this. The nobleLiu Beiis a descendant of the imperial house. He is of lofty virtue and great talent. Let him then take over the rule of this district and only too willingly I shall retire to have leisure to nurse my health.”

Liu Beireplied, “I came at the request ofKong Wenjubecause it was the right thing to do.Xu provinceis saved, but if I take it surely the world will say I am a wicked man.”

Mi Zhusaid, “You may not refuse. TheHouse of Hanis failing, their realm is crumbling and now is the time for doughty deeds and signal services. This is a fertile district, well populated, and you are the man to rule over it.”

“But I dare not accept.”

“TheGrand Administratoris a great sufferer,” saidChen Deng, “and cannot see to matters. You may not decline, Sir.”

SaidXuande, “Yuan Shubelongs to a family of rulers and the highest offices of state were held four times in three generations. The whole empire respects him, why not invite him to this task?”

“Because he is a rotting bone in a dark tomb; not worth talking about. This opportunity is a gift from Heaven and you will never cease to regret its loss,” saidKong Rong.

So spakeKong Rong, but stillLiu Beiobstinately refused.Tao Qianbesought him with tears. “I shall die if you leave me and there will be none to close my eyes.”

“Brother, you should accept the offer thus made,” saidGuan Yu.

“Why so much fuss?” saidZhang Fei. “We have not taken the place; it is he who wishes to give it you.”

“You all persuade me to do what is wrong,” saidLiu Bei.

Thrice didTao QianentreatLiu Beiand thrice was he refused. Then he said, “As he is set in his determination perhaps he will consent to encamp atXiapi. It is only a little town, but thence he can keep watch and ward over this city.”

They all with one voice prayedLiu Beito consent so he gave in. The feast of victory being now ended the time came to say farewell. WhenZhao Yuntook his leaveLiu Beiheld his hands alternately while dashing away the falling tears.Kong Rongand his leader went home to their own place.

WhenLiu Beiand his brothers took up their abode inXiapi, they first repaired the defenses and then they put out proclamations in order to calm the inhabitants.

Meanwhile,Cao Caowas leading his army back into his own territory whenCao Renarrived to inform him of all that had happened: thatLü Buled a mighty army with the assistance ofChen Gong, and that Yanzhou and Puyang had already been lost and only Juancheng, Dong’a, and Fan were still holding out to the very last, thanks to the combined cunning ofXun YuandCheng Yu.

“From what I know of the man,”Cao Caodeclared, “Lü Bumay be audacious but he has no guile. He’s not worth the worry.” And he issued orders for his army to make camp there while he summoned his generals for a council of war.

OnceLü Bulearned thatCao Caowas leading his army back and had already passed Teng county, he summoned two of his captains,Xue LanandLi Feng, and told them, “I’ve been meaning to put you two to use. Take ten thousand soldiers and hold Yanzhou; I’m going to lead the troops forward to routCao Cao.”

They assented. Yet thenChen Gongrushed in to say, “General, where are you going that you would give up Yanzhou?”

“I aim to camp troops at Puyang,”Lü Bureplied, “so as to putCao Caobetween two fires.”

“We can do better,” saidChen Gong. “You should indeed haveXue Lanremain here to hold Yanzhou. Yet a hundred and eighty li due south of here, there is a treacherous road through Taishan; you ought to assign ten thousand troops to post an ambush there. Having heard that Yanzhou has fallen,Cao Caois certain to led a hasty march along that road; if you wait until half the army has passed and then strike, you can capture his army in a single blow.”

YetLü Buonly replied, “My camping at Puyang will work just as well. What makes you assume failure?”

And spurningChen Gong’s advice, he leftXue Lanto hold Yanzhou and marched away.

Soon enough, asCao Caowas approaching that very road through Taishan,Guo Jiaremarked, “We had better halt; I fear there will be an ambush here.” YetCao Caolaughed and said, “As brainless a man asLü Buwill have only instructedXue Lanto hold Yanzhou while he marches for Puyang. How could he have laid an ambush here, eh?” And he orderedCao Rento take part of the army to besiege Yanzhou, while he led the rest on a swift march to attackLü Buat Puyang.

WhenChen Gongheard thatCao Cao’s forces were drawing near, he offered another plan, saying, “Cao Cao’s soldiers will be worn out after having marched so far. Better for us to offer battle at once rather than let them recover their strength and morale.”

YetLü Bureplied, “Why should I, who can ride roughshod over the whole length and breadth of the land, be anxious aboutCao Cao? Let him settle into his camp, then I’ll capture him myself.”

ThusCao Caoapproached Puyang and pitched camp nearby. The next day, he led his troops out and drew them up into formation in the field. Sitting astride his horse beneath the banners at the gate of his formation,Cao Caolooked on asLü Bu’s troops came up as well; they were fifty thousand all told, and the sound of their drums shook the field. They formed up into a circle, andLü Buhimself rode out in front, flanked by two of his captains: the first of these was a native of Mayi county in Yanmen commandery,Zhang LiaostyledWenyuan, while the other wasZang BastyledXuangao. These two were likewise followed by six others:Hao Meng,Cao Xing,Cheng Lian,Wei Xu,Song Xian, andHou Cheng.

Pointing to his opponent,Cao Caodemanded, “You and I had no quarrel; why then have you stolen my province and my commanderies?”

“All men are entitled to a share of Han’s castles,” retortedLü Bu. “Why should you get to keep them all?”

And at once he called forZang Bato ride forth and offer a challenge. FromCao Cao’s side,Yue Jinrode out to accept the duel. The two steeds approached each other, two spears were lifted both together and they exchanged more than thirty bouts with no advantage to either. ThenXiahou Dunslapped his horse and rode out to help his colleague while out wentZhang LiaofromLü Bu’s side to cut him off, and they fought a deadly duel as well.

Now that his own blood was up,Lü Butoo hefted his halberd and urged forward his steed to charge towards the enemy formation;Xiahou DunandYue Jinboth fled asLü Buswept forward, andCao Cao’s army was greatly defeated. They retreated thirty or forty li, whileLü Bubrought his own troops back to camp.

Having lost one battle,Cao Caoreturned to camp to hold another council with his generals.Yu Jinsaid, “From atop the hills today, I noticed thatLü Buhas another camp west of Puyang, yet with scarcely any troops; tonight they are sure to assume that we have fled far away in defeat and will not prepare any defenses. Why not lead soldiers to attack that camp? If we can take it,Lü Bu’s army is sure to be shaken. That would be the best plan.”

Cao Caofollowed his advice. He brought twenty thousand picked horse and foot, under the command ofCao Hong,Li Dian,Mao Jie,Lü Qian,Yu Jin, andDian Wei, and set out that very night along a side path to launch an attack against the camp

Meanwhile,Lü Buwas congratulating his men at his main camp.Chen Gongtold him, “The west camp is a vital location; shouldCao Caolaunch a surprise attack against it, what will you do?”

Lü Bureplied, “He’s already lost one battle today; would he really dare to come again?”

“Cao Caois a most able commander,”Chen Gonginsisted. “We must take measures against him attacking where we are not prepared.” ThusLü BuhadGao ShuntakeWei XuandHou Chengto lead troops to guard the west camp.

Already by dusk,Cao Caohad led troops to the western camp and had launched an assault from all sides; unable to endure the attack, the camp soldiers had scattered and fled, andCao Caohad taken possession of the camp. Not until the fourth watch of the night didGao Shunarrive there with his reinforcements. He led them charging forward, just asCao Caowas leading his own troops out again; the two sides ran into each other and fought a confused melee. Then just as dawn was breaking, from due west came the great shaking of drums, and men reported thatLü Buhimself had led his soldiers to the area.Cao Caothus abandoned the camp to fall back.

From behind,Gao ShunledWei XuandHou Chengto chase afterCao Cao’s troops; from ahead,Lü Buled the rest of his forces to press towards them. WhenYu JinandYue Jinattacking from two sides still could not checkLü Bu’s advance,Cao Caoturned away towards the north. Yet another force marched out from behind a hill, underZhang Liaoon the left andZang Baon the right.Cao CaobadeLü QianandCao Hongfight them off, but when the fighting went against them,Cao Caochanged directions again and fled towards the west. Suddenly there was another great clamor as still another band of troops appeared, forHao Meng,Cao Xing,Cheng Lian, andSong Xianhad barred this way out.

NowCao Cao’s soldiers were fighting for dear life, withCao Caoleading them to rush towards the enemy lines. But with a clatter of sticks, the enemy archers began to fire volleys of arrows thick as rain at them. Unable to advance yet out of ideas for getting away,Cao Caoexclaimed, “Someone save me!”

One of his generals dashed out on his horse; it wasDian Wei, wielding a pair of iron lances. “Fear not, my lord!” he called out. And he leaped from his steed, thrust his lances in the ground, and took out a dozen short blades which he held in his hand. He turned his head towards his followers and said, “When the ruffians are at ten paces from me, call it out.”

Then he set off with mighty strides, walking forward heedless of the flying arrows. Several dozen ofLü Bu’s horsemen thus rode towards him, and as they approached, the followers shouted, “Ten paces!”

“Five, then call!” shouted backDian Wei.

Presently, “Five paces!”

ThenDian Weiflung the blades. With every fling, a man fell from the saddle, and never a dart missed.

In mere moments about a dozen of the riders lay dead, while the remainder ran away.Dian Weiquickly remounted his steed, set his pair of lances, and charged into the enemy. Unable to endure,Lü Bu’s four generals each ran away. Now thatDian Weihad carved a path through the enemy army and helpedCao Caoto get out, onceCao Cao’s other generals and soldiers caught up as well, they followed the road back towards their own camp.

Yet just as they were watching the sky turn dim, a shout arose from behind them asLü Bucame galloping after them, his halberd in hand. “Stop right there, you Cao bandits!”

By then the men were so exhausted and their horses were so weary that, looking aghast at one another, every one of them would have bolted then and there.

And so it was:

Though you slip from out the snare,

The hunter still you must beware.

WouldCao Caosurvive this encounter? The next chapter will relate.
